title: Topic 250: Why Short Games Feel Like Ripoffs: Value Perception in Gaming
content:
# Topic 250: Why Short Games Feel Like Ripoffs: Value Perception in Gaming

## ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the curious case of the gamer's lament: the palpable sense of betrayal experienced when a full-priced video game concludes with the brevity of a summer romance. We explore the psychological underpinnings of value perception in the digital age, where the traditional "dollars-per-hour" metric collides with artistic ambition, narrative density, and the ever-present fear of having been thoroughly bamboozled. Through a rigorous, albeit hilarious, analysis of economic principles, psychological phenomena such as cognitive dissonance and price anchoring, and a smattering of real-world examples (including the infamous "five-hour masterpiece"), we seek to understand why a short, brilliant game can feel like a highway robbery, while a bloated, mediocre epic is often met with a resigned sigh. This groundbreaking study will not only illuminate the complex relationship between price, playtime, and player satisfaction but also provide a much-needed academic framework for complaining about video games on the internet.

## INTRODUCTION

In the grand bazaar of digital entertainment, where a king’s ransom can be exchanged for a fleeting glimpse of virtual glory, a peculiar and persistent grievance echoes through the hallowed halls of online forums and social media screeds: “The game was too short!” This cri de cœur, often accompanied by a string of enraged emojis and the digital equivalent of a shaken fist, is not merely the whimper of a petulant child denied their dessert. It is, we argue, a profound expression of a complex psychological and economic calculus that governs our perception of value in an increasingly intangible world. The modern gamer, a discerning consumer armed with a backlog of unplayed titles and a healthy dose of skepticism, approaches each new purchase with a mental spreadsheet, meticulously weighing the cost of admission against the promised hours of escapism. When the credits roll on a $60 epic after a mere handful of hours, the resulting sense of indignation can be as potent as any real-world injustice. This paper will venture into the heart of this gamer’s gambit, exploring the fascinating interplay of price, playtime, and perceived worth. We will dissect the psychological tripwires that transform a brief but brilliant experience into a perceived “ripoff,” and we will examine the economic forces that have trained us to equate length with value. Prepare, dear reader, for a journey into the minds of those who have paid full price for a five-hour game and lived to tweet about it. The results, we assure you, are as entertaining as they are enlightening.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

Our noble quest to understand the gamer’s psyche is not without precedent. The hallowed halls of academia have, in their infinite wisdom, occasionally turned their gaze towards the flickering screens of our digital playgrounds. A seminal, if slightly dry, study by Ballou et al. (2025) stumbled upon a truth that many a seasoned gamer knows in their bones: the sheer number of hours poured into a game does not, in fact, correlate with one’s overall happiness [1]. Instead, it is the *perceived value* of that time, the subjective sense that one’s digital dalliances are a worthwhile endeavor, that truly matters. This finding, while groundbreaking in its academic context, is met with a knowing nod by anyone who has spent 100 hours grinding for a slightly shinier virtual sword, only to feel a profound emptiness in their soul.

Further illuminating our path is the work of Johnston (2022), who, in a moment of journalistic brilliance, dared to ask the forbidden question: “Are video games too expensive?” [2]. His informal survey revealed that a significant portion of the gaming populace feels that, yes, they are indeed being fleeced. This is not merely a matter of sticker shock; it is a complex negotiation of expectations. The modern gamer, unlike their predecessors who faced a monolithic pricing structure, is adrift in a sea of variable costs, from the budget-friendly indie darling to the wallet-emptying AAA blockbuster. This price variation, Johnston argues, has forced us to become amateur economists, constantly calculating the “utility” of our purchases with the precision of a Wall Street quant. The concept of **price anchoring**, a well-documented psychological phenomenon, is particularly relevant here. A high price tag creates an expectation of a premium experience, and when that experience is shorter than a Netflix miniseries, the resulting cognitive dissonance can be jarring. As one Redditor so eloquently put it, “If I pay 5 bucks for a game I am far more forgiving of poor UI, QA problems, even game-breaking bugs… But if I’m having those same issues in a full-priced game it *really* feels like a kick in the teeth” [3]. This sentiment captures the essence of our inquiry: the perceived value of a game is not an absolute, but rather a delicate dance between what we pay and what we expect in return.

## ANALYSIS

At the heart of the “short game ripoff” phenomenon lies a fascinating psychological tug-of-war. The modern gamer, a creature of habit and high expectations, has been conditioned by decades of market trends to equate length with value. This “dollars-per-hour” metric, while seemingly logical, is a crude and often misleading measure of a game’s worth. It is a relic of a bygone era, a time when games were simpler, and the primary selling point was the sheer volume of “content.” Yet, it persists in our collective consciousness, a stubborn ghost in the machine of our consumer brains.

This is where **cognitive dissonance** rears its ugly head. When a player shells out $60 for a game, they are making a significant investment, not just of money, but of hope. They are buying into the promise of a grand adventure, a sprawling world, a narrative that will captivate them for weeks to come. When that adventure concludes in a brisk five hours, a chasm opens between their expectation and their reality. The resulting discomfort, the gnawing feeling of having been duped, is the very essence of cognitive dissonance. To resolve this internal conflict, the player must either devalue their investment (“The game was a ripoff”) or rationalize their purchase (“It was short, but it was a *masterpiece*”). The former is, by far, the more common and cathartic response.

The principle of **price anchoring** further exacerbates this issue. A high price tag serves as a powerful anchor, setting a high bar for the perceived quality and quantity of the experience. A $60 game is not just a game; it is a statement. It is a promise of a premium, polished, and, above all, *substantial* product. When that product fails to deliver on the “substantial” front, the anchor becomes a dead weight, dragging the player’s perception of value down into the murky depths of disappointment. The game may be a work of art, a technical marvel, a narrative tour de force, but if it is a *short* work of art, it is often judged not on its merits, but on its failure to meet the anchored expectation of length.

Furthermore, we must consider the **endowment effect**, a psychological principle that states that we place a higher value on things we own. When we purchase a game, it becomes a part of our digital library, a trophy on our virtual shelf. If that trophy feels insubstantial, if it represents a mere handful of hours of entertainment, our sense of ownership is diminished. We feel as though we have acquired not a treasured possession, but a fleeting experience, a digital mayfly that lives and dies in the span of an afternoon. This, in turn, fuels the perception of being ripped off. We have not bought a game; we have merely rented one at an exorbitant price.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The annals of video game history are littered with the corpses of short games that were crucified in the court of public opinion. One of the most infamous examples is *The Order: 1886*, a PlayStation 4 exclusive that was lauded for its stunning graphics and cinematic presentation, but lambasted for its five-hour campaign. The game, which was sold at a full $60 price point, became a poster child for the “style over substance” critique. The internet erupted in a firestorm of outrage, with players feeling that they had paid for a feature-length film rather than a full-fledged game. The game’s developers, Ready at Dawn, defended their creation, arguing that they had focused on quality over quantity, but the damage was done. The game’s short length became its defining feature, a cautionary tale whispered among gamers for years to come.

Another classic case is *Metal Gear Solid V: Ground Zeroes*, a prologue to the much larger *Metal Gear Solid V: The Phantom Pain*. *Ground Zeroes* was a polished and replayable experience, but its main story mission could be completed in under two hours. The game was released as a standalone product for $30, a price that many felt was far too high for what was essentially a glorified demo. The backlash was so severe that the game’s price was quickly reduced. This incident highlights the importance of managing player expectations. Had *Ground Zeroes* been marketed as a low-priced appetizer to the main course of *The Phantom Pain*, it might have been received more favorably. Instead, it was perceived as a cynical cash grab, a blatant attempt to charge a premium for a sliver of content.

On the other end of the spectrum, we have games like *Journey*, a critically acclaimed indie title that can be completed in a single sitting. *Journey* was released at a modest price point, and its short length was seen not as a flaw, but as a feature. The game’s brevity allowed its emotional arc to land with maximum impact, creating a concise and unforgettable experience. This demonstrates that short games can be successful, but only if their price and marketing are aligned with their length. The developers of *Journey*, thatgamecompany, understood that they were not selling a 100-hour epic, but a two-hour poem. They priced their game accordingly, and the rest is history.

## DISCUSSION

The curious case of the short game “ripoff” offers a fascinating window into the evolving landscape of consumer behavior in the digital age. The passionate, and often vitriolic, reactions to short, full-priced games reveal a fundamental tension between the traditional, quantity-based metrics of value and the more nebulous, quality-based metrics of artistic merit. This is not merely a debate about video games; it is a microcosm of a larger cultural shift in how we consume and value digital content.

The insights gleaned from this analysis have profound implications for our understanding of human consumer behavior. They demonstrate that our perception of value is not a rational, objective calculation, but a deeply subjective and emotional experience, shaped by a complex interplay of psychological biases, market conditioning, and personal expectations. The modern consumer is not a passive recipient of goods and services, but an active and often demanding participant in the marketplace, armed with a wealth of information and a powerful public platform to voice their grievances. The “short game” controversy is a testament to this new reality, a world where the consumer is not always right, but is always heard.

For the creators and purveyors of digital content, the lessons are clear. Transparency, communication, and a keen understanding of player psychology are no longer optional; they are essential for survival. The days of the one-size-fits-all pricing model are numbered. The future, it seems, belongs to those who can successfully navigate the treacherous waters of player expectation, who can strike a delicate balance between artistic ambition and perceived value, and who can convince the discerning consumer that their five-hour masterpiece is not a ripoff, but a bargain.

## CONCLUSION

In the final analysis, the outcry over short games is not merely the grumbling of entitled gamers; it is a rational, if emotionally charged, response to a perceived violation of an implicit contract between creator and consumer. We have been trained, by decades of marketing and market forces, to expect a certain amount of “bang for our buck.” When that expectation is not met, the resulting sense of betrayal is as real as any financial loss. The short game, in its brilliant brevity, challenges our preconceived notions of value, forcing us to confront the uncomfortable truth that not all that is good is long, and not all that is long is good. As the digital marketplace continues to evolve, so too will our understanding of what it means to get our money’s worth. In the meantime, let us raise a glass to the short game, the misunderstood martyr of the digital age, and may its creators find solace in the knowledge that their work, however brief, has sparked a truly fascinating, and often hilarious, debate.
references:
[1] Ballou, N., Vuorre, M., Hakman, T., Magnusson, K., & Przybylski, A. K. (2025). Perceived value of video games, but not hours played, predicts mental well-being in casual adult Nintendo players. R Soc Open Sci., 12(3), 241174. https://doi.org/10.1098/rsos.241174

[2] Johnston, A. (2022, May 20). A Fistful of Coins: The Psychology of Game Pricing. Superjump. https://www.superjumpmagazine.com/a-fistful-of-coins-the-psychology-of-game-pricing/

[3] Various Authors. (2022, March 4). Does the price of a game affect it’s perceived value, if so why? [Online forum post]. Reddit. https://www.reddit.com/r/gamedev/comments/1b5y5i1/does_the_price_of_a_game_affect_its_perceived/
